text: This is our single anastomosis with duodenal ileostomy and sleeve gastrectomy otherwise known as CDS presentation.

Hello, I'm Dr. Daniel Cottom and we've received many requests from our patients to have a full video of our CDS also known as our lube duodenal switch procedure. performed and put out on the internet.
Here we're going to, with the following video, show you how we do the looped abdominal switch in our daily practice, and this is true for both Dr. Medlin and I. I hope you enjoy the video.
The surgeon locates the ileocecal valve, which is the anatomical junction where the colon meets the small intestine.
The surgeon locates the ileocecal valve and traces the small bowel backward to identify an appropriate site for the connection between the duodenum and the ileostomy.

Usually this is three hundred centimeters from the ileocecal valve, but it could be more or less depending on the size of the patient and the muscle mass of the patient or pre-existing conditions that need to be considered.
The surgeon secures the identified section of the bowel to a piece of adjacent fat tissue to ensure it can be easily located later for the second part of the operation.

The surgeon uses a cauterizing device to take down blood vessels connecting the arteries and fat to the stomach. The device seals and cuts the vessels simultaneously, providing a dry surgical field while dissecting the stomach.
The surgeon completes dissection of the stomach up to the top and proceeds to dissect the duodenum at the bottom part of the stomach, approaching the pylorus.
The surgeon encounters and dissects through filmy adhesions posteriorly to access the duodenum. A marker is placed to visually distinguish where the stomach ends and the small bowel begins.

The surgeon identifies the pylorus, the valve at the end of the stomach, and the duodenum below it. A window is created through the fat layer to allow placement of a stapler. A standard bariatric clamp is then placed inside the abdomen.

The surgeon places the stomach through a standard bariatric clamp, using a guiding device to assist in excising the stomach. The device simultaneously cuts and staples the tissue, securing the excision.

The surgeon uses a gastrointestinal anastomosis (GIA) stapler, positioning it adjacent to the bariatric clamp to create a straight staple line along the stomach, aiding in the prevention of sleeve strictures.

The surgeon completes the sleeve gastrectomy and uses a liver retractor to move the liver aside, providing clear access to the duodenum. A GIA stapler is positioned beyond the pylorus to cut across the duodenum, and reinforcement material is removed from the stapler device.
The surgeon removes the tacking stitch and proceeds to attach the proximal duodenum to the distal loop limb, identified as the ileum, using surgical instruments.

The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to connect the cut edge of the duodenum on the right to the small bowel loop limb on the left, ensuring the connection is free of tension. The sutures are placed along the cut edge, with careful attention to remain close to the fat layer while penetrating the small bowel.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to create holes on either side of the duodenum and ileum, opening windows in the tissue without causing bleeding.
The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to sew the posterior layer of the duodenum and small bowel loop limb together, creating a secure connection between the two halves.

The surgeon completes a running stitch on the right side of the duodenum, visible as a continuous suture line. They begin another suture to close the anterior portion using a baseball stitch technique, ensuring the opening is securely tied off.
The surgeon uses an endo stitch device to hand sew the connection between the proximal duodenum and the distal loop limb (ileum), ensuring precise alignment and secure attachment of the tissues.
The surgeon places an anti-obstruction stitch to ensure food travels down the correct side of the loop channel, preventing potential symptoms from incorrect food passage.
The surgeon places a second anti-obstruction stitch to ensure there is no tension on the connection points within the loop channel.
The surgeon inflates both the stomach and the small bowel with air to check for leaks in the hand-sewn connection, observing the absence of bubbles indicating a secure closure.

After completing the air leak test, the surgeon dilates the abdominal wall and inserts a large clamp into the abdomen. The stomach is then extracted through one of the small incisions, demonstrating its distensible nature.
This is a diagram of what we've just done. This is the SADI-S. So we have the sleeve gastrectomy and the small bowel connection there illustrated.

Our average operative time is approximately seventy minutes and people who have this done usually stay one day in the hospital or just an overnight stay in the surgical center. Thank you for paying attention to us. Have a good day.
